Multiple perspectives, more comprehensive, more robust.

Q104 CONCEPT
What are the core technologies behind RAG-Fusion? (Multi-channel retrieval + answer fusion)
CORE TECHNOLOGY 1 — QUERY GENERATION

An LLM takes your original question and generates 3–5 different versions of it — different phrasings, different angles, different specificity levels.

Query Generation Example
Original: "How do I return a product?" Generated queries: Q1: "What is the return policy?" Q2: "How can I get a refund for a purchase?" Q3: "Product return process step by step" Q4: "Can I exchange a defective item?" Q5: "Return shipping instructions"
CORE TECHNOLOGY 2 — RECIPROCAL RANK FUSION (RRF)

RRF is the algorithm that combines rankings from multiple retrieval runs into one unified ranking. It rewards documents that appear consistently across multiple searches.

RRF Score for a document = Σ (1 / (k + rank_in_each_list)) where k is a constant (usually 60). Documents that rank well in multiple searches get high RRF scores.
CORE TECHNOLOGY 3 — ANSWER FUSION (SYNTHESIS)

After getting a unified document list, instead of just picking top-K and sending to the LLM, RAG-Fusion can also generate partial answers from each sub-query and then have the LLM synthesize them into a final answer — reducing lost information.

RRF Scoring — How Documents Rise to the Top
Retrieval from Q1: Doc_A(rank 1), Doc_B(rank 2), Doc_C(rank 5) Retrieval from Q2: Doc_C(rank 1), Doc_A(rank 3), Doc_D(rank 2) Retrieval from Q3: Doc_A(rank 1), Doc_C(rank 2), Doc_E(rank 4) RRF Score (k=60): Doc_A: 1/(60+1) + 1/(60+3) + 1/(60+1) = 0.0164+0.0154+0.0164 = 0.0482 Doc_C: 1/(60+5) + 1/(60+1) + 1/(60+2) = 0.0154+0.0164+0.0159 = 0.0477 Doc_A wins! It consistently ranked high across multiple queries ✅
Q105 WORKFLOW
Walk me through the RAG-Fusion workflow.
COMPLETE STEP-BY-STEP WORKFLOW
1
Receive User QueryUser asks: "What medications should I avoid with blood thinners?"
2
Generate Multiple Sub-Queries (LLM)LLM generates 4 diverse phrasings: "Drug interactions with anticoagulants," "Foods and medicines to avoid on warfarin," "Contraindications for blood thinner medications," "NSAIDs and anticoagulant risks."
3
Parallel Retrieval for All QueriesRun all 5 queries (original + 4 generated) simultaneously against the knowledge base. Each returns top-10 documents — total of ~50 candidate documents.
4
Apply RRF FusionScore all ~50 documents using Reciprocal Rank Fusion. Documents appearing in multiple query results score higher. De-duplicate identical documents.
5
Select Top-K Fused DocumentsTake the top 10 highest-scoring documents from the fused, re-ranked list.
6
Generate Final AnswerSend original query + top-10 documents to the LLM. It generates a comprehensive, accurate answer with citations.
Figure 105.1 — Complete RAG-Fusion Pipeline: Query fan-out → Parallel retrieval → RRF fusion → LLM answer
Q106 CONCEPT
What are the advantages and disadvantages of RAG-Fusion?
✅ Advantages
  • More complete answers — catches documents missed by a single query
  • More robust — works even when user's phrasing is unusual
  • Better ranking — documents consistently ranked high across queries are more likely genuinely relevant
  • Reduces vocabulary gap — multiple query phrasings cover synonyms naturally
  • Higher recall — finds relevant docs a single query would miss
❌ Disadvantages
  • Slower — 5 retrievals instead of 1 (though can be parallelized)
  • More expensive — extra LLM calls to generate sub-queries
  • Complex to implement — RRF scoring, deduplication, parallelization
  • Diminishing returns — for simple factual queries, standard RAG is sufficient
  • Noisy sub-queries — poorly generated sub-queries can pollute results
Use RAG-Fusion when: questions are complex, multi-faceted, or when users' phrasings are unpredictable. Use standard RAG when: questions are simple, factual, and your vocabulary is controlled.
Q107 CONCEPT
What are the optimization strategies for RAG modules (Retrieval, Generation, Storage)?
MODULE 1 — RETRIEVAL OPTIMIZATION
StrategyDescription
Better ChunkingSemantic chunking (split at paragraph/sentence boundaries, not character limits)
Hybrid SearchCombine dense vectors + sparse BM25 for best of both worlds
Re-rankingUse a cross-encoder model to re-rank top-50 results
Query RewritingUse LLM to improve poorly phrased queries before retrieval
HyDEGenerate hypothetical ideal answer, embed it, search for similar docs
MODULE 2 — GENERATION OPTIMIZATION
StrategyDescription
Better PromptingClear instructions to "answer only from provided documents," "cite your sources"
Context CompressionBefore sending to LLM, compress/summarize chunks to fit more information in context window
Chain-of-ThoughtAsk LLM to reason step-by-step, not just answer directly
Temperature TuningLower temperature (0.1–0.3) for factual RAG answers to reduce randomness
Smaller, Faster ModelUse a cheaper LLM for simple retrievals, expensive model only when needed
MODULE 3 — STORAGE OPTIMIZATION
StrategyDescription
HNSW IndexApproximate nearest-neighbor algorithm — much faster than exact search at scale
QuantizationCompress vector floats from 32-bit to 8-bit — 4x less storage, similar accuracy
CachingCache embeddings for frequently queried documents, cache final answers for repeated questions
Tiered StorageHot data (frequently accessed) in fast memory, cold data on cheap disk
Incremental UpdatesOnly re-embed changed documents, don't rebuild entire index
Q108 ARCHITECTURE
How would you architect a RAG system for production? (caching, streaming, modular deployment)
A production RAG system is like a well-run restaurant kitchen — not just one chef (the AI) but a whole team: a host (API gateway), waiters (load balancers), prep cooks (document processors), a head chef (LLM), a sous chef (retriever), and a dishwasher (cache cleaner). Everything works together smoothly.
Production RAG Architecture — Full System Design
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ CLIENT / UI │ └──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘ │ HTTPS ┌──────────────────────▼──────────────────────────────┐ │ API GATEWAY + LOAD BALANCER │ │ (Rate limiting, Auth, Request routing) │ └────────┬──────────────────────────────┬─────────────┘ │ │ ┌────────▼─────────┐ ┌─────────▼────────────┐ │ QUERY CACHE │ │ RAG ORCHESTRATOR │ │ (Redis/Memcached)│ │ (LangChain/Custom) │ │ Check if answer │ └──┬──────────┬─────────┘ │ already cached │ │ │ └──────────────────┘ ┌────────▼──┐ ┌────▼──────────┐ │ RETRIEVER │ │ LLM Service │ │ (Pinecone │ │ (GPT-4/Claude)│ │ /Weaviate)│ │ + Streaming │ └───────────┘ └───────────────┘ │ ┌──────────▼──────────┐ │ VECTOR STORE │ │ (HNSW Index) │ │ + BM25 Index │ └─────────────────────┘ ▲ ┌──────────┴──────────┐ │ DOCUMENT PIPELINE │ │ Extract → Chunk → │ │ Embed → Index │ └─────────────────────┘
KEY PRODUCTION COMPONENTS
  • Semantic Caching: If two users ask very similar questions, return the cached answer — huge cost savings. Use Redis with vector similarity on the query embedding.
  • Streaming Responses: Don't wait for the full answer — stream tokens as the LLM generates them. Users see text appearing in real-time, feels much faster.
  • Observability: Log every retrieval (what docs were fetched), every generation (what the LLM output), latency at each step. Use tools like LangSmith, Arize, or Prometheus.
  • Modular Deployment: Each component (retriever, LLM, embedding model) is a separate microservice — can be upgraded independently without rebuilding everything.
  • Fallback Chain: Primary LLM fails → fallback to smaller model → if still fails → return "I don't know" gracefully.
Q109 CONCEPT
How do you optimize RAG indexing? (embedding quality, incremental updates, deduplication)
1. EMBEDDING QUALITY

Your embedding model is the lens through which documents are "understood." A blurry lens = poor retrieval.

  • Use state-of-the-art embedding models (e.g., OpenAI text-embedding-3-large, Cohere Embed, or open-source BGE, E5)
  • Fine-tune embeddings on your domain data for best results
  • Use longer context embeddings for longer documents (not just 512 tokens)
  • Test different models using retrieval benchmarks (MTEB, BEIR) on your own data
2. INCREMENTAL UPDATES

You don't want to re-index 1 million documents every time one document changes. Use incremental indexing:

1
Hash Every DocumentCalculate a checksum (SHA-256) for each document when first indexed. Store hash alongside embedding.
2
Detect ChangesWhen a document is updated, compare new hash with stored hash. If different → re-embed and update.
3
Delete Stale EmbeddingsRemove old embeddings for deleted documents to prevent retrieving outdated information.
4
Background ProcessingRun indexing updates as background jobs (not blocking user requests) using a queue like Celery or AWS SQS.
3. DEDUPLICATION

The same information often appears in multiple documents — meeting notes + email summaries + Slack messages might all say the same thing. Deduplication prevents retrieving the same content 5 times.

  • Exact dedup: Hash comparison — identical documents removed
  • Near-dedup: MinHash/LSH — near-identical documents (>90% similar) merged
  • Semantic dedup: Embedding similarity — semantically redundant chunks removed
Q110 CONCEPT
How do you optimize RAG index data? (format normalization, redundancy removal, text normalization)
Imagine your address book has entries in 10 different formats: "New Delhi, India", "New Delhi", "Delhi", "N. Delhi, IN", "delhi india". When you search "Delhi," you should find all of them. Text normalization makes all versions consistent so nothing is missed.
DATA QUALITY PIPELINE BEFORE INDEXING
1
Format NormalizationConvert all documents to a consistent format: UTF-8 encoding, plain text or Markdown. Remove PDF formatting artifacts, HTML tags, escape characters.
2
Text NormalizationLowercase (or keep case for proper nouns), normalize whitespace (tabs/newlines → single space), normalize punctuation, expand abbreviations ("Q1" → "Quarter 1").
3
Redundancy RemovalRemove boilerplate (headers, footers, disclaimers that repeat across all documents). Remove duplicate sections within documents (same paragraph copy-pasted).
4
Quality FilteringRemove chunks that are: too short (<50 words — likely meaningless snippets), too repetitive (same sentence 10 times), mostly numbers/symbols, or low-entropy (random characters).
5
Metadata EnrichmentAdd useful metadata to each chunk: source document name, date created, section/chapter, author, document type, access permissions. Use during retrieval for filtering.
Q111 FUTURE
What is the future direction of RAG? (Multimodal, Agent-driven retrieval, Online fine-tuning)
FUTURE DIRECTION 1 — MULTIMODAL RAG

Current RAG retrieves text. Future RAG will retrieve everything:

  • Images: "Show me the circuit diagram from page 12" — retrieve actual image chunks
  • Video: Retrieve specific 30-second clips from a 2-hour training video
  • Audio: Retrieve podcast segments, meeting recordings
  • Charts/Graphs: Understand and retrieve visual data representations

Tools like GPT-4V, Gemini 1.5, and Claude's vision capabilities are making this possible now.

FUTURE DIRECTION 2 — AGENT-DRIVEN RETRIEVAL

Instead of a fixed retrieval pipeline, an AI agent decides how to retrieve:

  • Decides whether to search the vector DB, run a SQL query, call an API, or browse the web
  • Iteratively retrieves — if first search isn't enough, it decides to search again with different terms
  • Self-corrects — if the answer seems wrong, the agent retrieves more to verify

This is called Agentic RAG or Self-RAG — the AI is in the driver's seat of retrieval.

FUTURE DIRECTION 3 — GRAPH RAG

Instead of treating documents as isolated chunks, GraphRAG builds a knowledge graph — connecting entities and relationships:

GraphRAG vs Standard RAG
Standard RAG: "Apple Inc." ← independent chunk "Tim Cook" ← independent chunk "iPhone" ← independent chunk (No connections between them) GraphRAG: Apple Inc. ──[CEO]──→ Tim Cook │ └──[makes]──→ iPhone ──[runs]──→ iOS (Relationships allow multi-hop reasoning!) Query: "Who leads the company that makes iPhone?" Standard: might miss it GraphRAG: follows edges ✅
FUTURE DIRECTION 4 — ONLINE / CONTINUOUS LEARNING

Current RAG has a separate, offline indexing step. Future RAG will learn in real-time: when a user provides feedback ("that answer was wrong"), the system immediately updates weights — not just the document index. The model itself improves from usage.

Q112 CONCEPT
What are the limitations of RAG?
ALL KEY LIMITATIONS
LimitationDescriptionPartial Mitigation
Retrieval can failIf wrong documents are retrieved, answer will be wrong even with perfect LLMBetter embedding, hybrid search, re-ranking
Context window limitsCan only send so many documents to the LLM at onceCompression, map-reduce patterns
Latency overheadRetrieval adds 100ms–2s compared to direct LLM callCaching, HNSW indexes, streaming
Still can hallucinateLLM can misread or misinterpret retrieved documentsLower temperature, constrained generation
Multi-hop reasoningQuestions needing info from 3+ documents are hardMulti-step retrieval, GraphRAG
Structured data weaknessBad at SQL/math/aggregationHybrid RAG + NL2SQL
Security risksPrompt injection, privilege escalationRBAC, input sanitization
Knowledge update lagNew documents must be explicitly indexedReal-time indexing pipelines
Q113 CONCEPT
What are the key advantages of RAG?
WHY COMPANIES LOVE RAG
AdvantageBusiness Impact
No retraining neededAdd new knowledge by adding documents — 1 minute vs weeks of training
Works with private dataInternal policies, contracts, customer data — without exposing to external LLM training
Verifiable answersCan cite sources — critical for legal, medical, financial domains
Always currentUpdate documents daily, answers reflect latest info immediately
Cost effectiveUse a smaller LLM (cheaper) + great retrieval → better than expensive LLM alone
Modular and flexibleSwap out embedding model, vector DB, or LLM independently
Scales to billions of docsVector DB handles massive scale — LLM only ever sees top-10 docs
Reduces hallucinationsGrounded answers from real documents, not AI imagination
Q114 FUTURE
What technologies might RAG integrate with in the future? (GraphRAG, Self-RAG, AgentRAG)
INTEGRATION 1 — GRAPHRAG (Microsoft)

Combines knowledge graphs (entities and relationships) with vector retrieval. Enables multi-hop reasoning: "Find the CEO of the parent company of the startup that acquired company X." Standard RAG can't chain these relationships — GraphRAG can follow edges in the graph.

INTEGRATION 2 — SELF-RAG

The LLM decides for itself whether to retrieve — and whether the retrieved content was useful:

  • If confident: answer directly without retrieval (saves time/cost)
  • If uncertain: trigger retrieval
  • After retrieval: critique whether retrieved docs actually helped
  • If not useful: retrieve again with better query

The system has self-awareness about when it needs help and when it doesn't.

INTEGRATION 3 — AGENT RAG

RAG embedded inside an AI agent that can use multiple retrieval tools:

  • Search internal vector DB
  • Query SQL databases
  • Browse the web
  • Call external APIs
  • Execute code and analyze results

The agent orchestrates which retrieval strategy to use based on the question type.

INTEGRATION 4 — MULTIMODAL RAG

Retrieve images, audio, video alongside text. Ask "Show me which product diagram was referenced in the safety incident report" — retrieves both the text passage and the actual diagram image.

Section 3 · Part C
Situation-Based & Tricky Problems
Questions 115–134 · Real-world scenarios, debugging, and expert reasoning
Q115 SITUATION
Your RAG system retrieves 10 relevant documents, but the LLM ignores document #3 which contains the correct answer. Why might this happen?
The Scenario
You retrieve docs 1–10. Doc #3 has the answer. But the LLM outputs the wrong answer, ignoring doc #3 entirely.
THE ROOT CAUSE — "LOST IN THE MIDDLE"

This is a well-documented phenomenon called the "Lost in the Middle" problem (Stanford research, 2023). LLMs pay more attention to information at the beginning and end of their context — information in the middle gets less attention.

LLM Attention Distribution Across Context Position
High attention ▲ │█ │█ █ │█ █ █ █ │█ █ █ █ █ █ █ │█ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ █ Low attention └─────────────────────────────────────────▶ Doc1 Doc2 Doc3 Doc4 Doc5 Doc6 Doc7 Doc8 Doc9 Doc10 ↑ Docs in the middle get LESS attention from LLM
ALL POSSIBLE REASONS + FIXES
ReasonFix
Lost in the MiddlePut most important docs first or last. Use "document reordering" — place the highest-scored doc at position 1.
Doc #3 is too longCompress/summarize doc #3 before sending. Shorter context = more attention per word.
Contradictory docsDocs 1, 2, 4-10 all say X, doc 3 says Y. LLM goes with majority. Use citation-forcing prompt.
Doc #3 poorly writtenIf doc #3 uses unusual language, LLM may struggle to parse it. Rewrite/clean source documents.
Prompt doesn't force reading all docsAdd: "Carefully read ALL provided documents before answering. Consider each one." to your prompt.
Best fix: Implement "relevance-ordered context" — always put the single most relevant document at position 1 in the context. Use your re-ranker's top result as context position #1.
Q116 TRICKY
You have 1 million documents. Your vector search returns the top 10. But the 11th document actually has the answer. How do you design retrieval to prevent missing the answer?
Imagine you're playing "guess the word" and you get 3 clues. With 3 clues you're confident, but the 4th clue would have made it obvious. The problem is you only get 3. RAG faces this too: what if the answer is just outside the top-K threshold?
WHY THIS HAPPENS

With 1 million documents, similarity scores between documents are often very close. Doc 10 might score 0.87 and doc 11 might score 0.869 — a meaningless difference, but only 10 get retrieved.

SOLUTIONS — HOW TO IMPROVE RECALL
1
Increase Top-K for Retrieval, then Re-rankRetrieve top-50 by fast vector search, then use an expensive-but-accurate cross-encoder re-ranker to select the best 10. Doc #11 (old ranking) might become doc #3 (new ranking).
2
Multi-Query Retrieval (RAG-Fusion)Generate 5 different queries. Each retrieves top-10. If doc #11 by query 1 is doc #8 by query 2, RRF fusion will surface it in the top-10 fused list.
3
Hybrid Search (Dense + Sparse)BM25 keyword search and vector search rank documents differently. A document scoring 11th on vectors might score 3rd on BM25 keywords — hybrid fusion catches it.
4
HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embedding)Ask LLM to write an ideal answer, embed that ideal answer, search for docs similar to the ideal answer. This "expected answer" query may rank doc 11 higher than the original question does.
5
Dynamic Top-KInstead of fixed top-10, retrieve until the similarity score drops below a threshold. If docs 1–12 all score > 0.85, retrieve all 12.
Q117 SITUATION
A user asks "What did my colleague John say about the Q3 budget?" The answer is in an email John sent, but your RAG system can't find it because the email mentions "John" only in the signature. How do you handle this?
THE ROOT PROBLEM

The email body might say "I recommend increasing the Q3 budget by 15%" — but "John" only appears at the bottom in "Best, John Smith." Vector search on the body text won't connect "John" to this email because the name isn't in the semantic content.

MULTI-LAYER SOLUTION
1
Metadata Extraction at Indexing TimeWhen indexing emails, extract structured metadata: sender name, sender email, recipients, date, subject. Store "John Smith" as the author metadata field.
2
Metadata-Filtered RetrievalWhen query mentions "John," first check if "John" maps to a known person in your directory. If yes, filter by author = "John Smith" before or during vector search.
3
Entity Recognition (NER)Run Named Entity Recognition on all documents at indexing time. Tag "John Smith" as a PERSON entity and link it to their metadata profile (email address, department, role).
4
Prepend Metadata to ChunksWhen creating the chunk for embedding, prepend: "From: John Smith | Subject: Q3 Budget Review | Date: 2024-09-15 | Content: [email body]" — now "John" is semantically linked to the content.
5
Person-Aware Query RewritingQuery rewriter detects "John" as a known colleague → rewrites query to "Q3 budget (author:john.smith@company.com)" and adds metadata filter.
Key insight: Always embed metadata alongside content. Never separate who-wrote-it from what-they-wrote.
Q118 TRICKY
Two documents: Doc A says "Take 5mg." Doc B (more recent) says "Take 10mg." Your RAG retrieves both. The LLM outputs "5mg." Is this a RAG problem or an LLM problem?
ANSWER: IT'S BOTH — AND THE ANSWER IS MORE NUANCED

This is a shared failure. Let's analyze each component:

🔍 RAG Component Failure
  • RAG did not signal which document is more recent
  • No temporal ordering of context presented to LLM
  • Metadata (date) not passed to LLM
  • Both docs treated as equally authoritative
🤖 LLM Component Failure
  • LLM chose the first (older) document without reasoning
  • Did not recognize the conflict to raise it
  • Did not ask "which is more authoritative?"
  • Lost in the Middle — Doc A came first in context
HOW TO FIX — COMPLETE SOLUTION
1
Pass Date Metadata in ContextFormat context as: "[Doc A — Dated 2022-01] Take 5mg. [Doc B — Dated 2024-03] Take 10mg." Now the LLM can see the dates.
2
Instruct LLM to Prefer Recent DocsIn system prompt: "When documents conflict, prefer the most recently dated document."
3
Conflict Detection LayerBefore sending to LLM, run a conflict detector: "Do any retrieved documents contradict each other?" If yes, flag it and handle specially — e.g., show both versions to the user with dates.
4
Version-Aware RetrievalWhen indexing, mark "superseded" documents. Don't retrieve them unless user specifically asks for historical data. Only show the current version by default.
In medical or legal contexts, serving outdated guidance (5mg instead of 10mg) is a patient safety issue. Always include dates in context and implement supersession policies for updated documents.
Q119 SITUATION
Your RAG system works great for English queries but fails for Spanish queries on the same English documents. What's wrong?
Imagine your library catalog is in English. You ask "corazón" (Spanish for heart) but the catalog only has "heart." Even though the book exists, the catalog can't find it because the search term and the catalog speak different languages.
ROOT CAUSES
  • Monolingual Embeddings: The embedding model was only trained on English text. Spanish queries produce embeddings in a completely different vector space — they don't align with the English document embeddings.
  • Cross-lingual Gap: "How to fix a broken heart" (English) and "Cómo arreglar un corazón roto" (Spanish) should be similar vectors — but in a monolingual model, they're far apart.
  • BM25 Keyword Failure: Spanish words literally don't match English words at all.
SOLUTIONS
1
Use Multilingual EmbeddingsReplace your embedding model with a multilingual one: multilingual-e5-large, paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2, or Cohere's multilingual model. These map equivalent concepts across 100+ languages to the same vector space.
2
Query Translation (Simplest Fix)Translate the Spanish query to English before retrieval using a translation API (DeepL, Google Translate, or LLM). Then run your existing English retrieval pipeline. Simple and effective.
3
Index Documents in Multiple LanguagesTranslate English documents to Spanish, French, etc. and index them. Now Spanish queries match Spanish document versions directly.
4
Language DetectionDetect query language → route to language-specific retrieval pipeline. Spanish queries → Spanish-aware retriever. English → English retriever.
Recommendation: For most production systems, solution #1 (multilingual embeddings) is the most robust. Solution #2 (translation) is the quickest to implement.
Q120 TRICKY
Your retrieval recall is 95%, but end-user satisfaction is only 60%. What other factors could explain this gap?
You gave a student 95% of the right study materials before their exam — but they still only scored 60%. Having the right materials doesn't guarantee understanding, presentation quality, or applying them correctly!
RETRIEVAL IS ONLY ONE PART — ALL THE OTHER FACTORS
FactorHow it Causes Dissatisfaction Despite Good Retrieval
Answer Quality (LLM)Right docs retrieved, but LLM writes a confusing, poorly-structured answer
Answer LengthUsers want a concise bullet-point answer, LLM writes 5 paragraphs (or vice versa)
Tone/Format MismatchTechnical answer when user wanted simple explanation, or too basic when expert wanted detail
LatencyCorrect answer but takes 8 seconds — users are frustrated and give low ratings
Hallucination in GenerationCorrect docs retrieved but LLM adds fabricated details not in the docs
Missing context across turnsCorrect answer for this question but doesn't connect to previous questions in conversation
UX/InterfaceGood answer but not displayed clearly (no formatting, no citations, wall of text)
Recall ≠ Precision95% recall means you found the right doc — but retrieved 20 docs total. LLM drowns in noise.
User Expectation GapUser wanted a task completed, not just information. RAG answered but didn't take action.
Key insight: End-to-end evaluation (user satisfaction) ≠ component evaluation (retrieval recall). Always measure the full pipeline, not just individual components. User satisfaction measures the whole experience.
Q121 SITUATION
A competitor claims their RAG system has 99% accuracy. You suspect overfitting. What questions would you ask to validate?
A student scores 100% on practice tests from the textbook — because they memorized the exact textbook questions. Put them in a real exam with new questions, and they score 60%. The 100% is misleading. "99% accuracy" for a RAG system can be just as misleading.
CRITICAL QUESTIONS TO ASK (SKEPTIC'S CHECKLIST)
1
"What dataset was accuracy measured on?"Was the test set drawn from the same documents as the training/indexing set? If yes → likely inflated. A fair evaluation uses held-out questions about documents the system has never seen.
2
"How was 'accuracy' defined?"Exact match? Semantic similarity? Human rating? "The answer contains the key fact" vs "the answer is fully correct" are very different standards. Easy metrics inflate scores.
3
"Were questions generated from the same documents?"If test questions were generated by GPT from the indexed documents, the system is effectively tested on its own training data. That's circular.
4
"How did it handle out-of-distribution questions?"Test it on questions where the answer is NOT in the knowledge base. A good system says "I don't know." A bad system hallucinates confidently.
5
"What was the evaluation methodology?"Was there a blind evaluation? Were evaluators the same people who built the system? Independent evaluation by outsiders is far more credible.
6
"What types of questions were included?"Easy factual lookups can hit 99%. Complex multi-hop, negation, comparative, and temporal reasoning questions are much harder. Were these included?
99% accuracy on a curated benchmark often drops to 70–80% in real-world usage. Always demand evaluation on real user queries from production logs, not synthetic benchmarks.
Q122 TRICKY
A single clause split across two chunks (512-token chunks) causes the LLM to miss a critical condition. How do you redesign chunking?
A legal contract says: "You are entitled to a refund... [chunk boundary here] ...unless the product was opened." If chunk 1 retrieves "entitled to a refund" and chunk 2 isn't retrieved, the AI says "yes you get a refund" — completely wrong because the critical "unless" clause was cut off.
THE CHUNKING REDESIGN SOLUTIONS
Chunking Strategy Comparison
STRATEGY 1 — Fixed Size (CURRENT, BAD) ├ Chunk 1 [512 tokens]: "...entitled to a refund..." └ Chunk 2 [512 tokens]: "...unless product was opened..." ❌ Clause split. Only chunk 1 retrieved. Wrong answer. STRATEGY 2 — Overlapping Chunks (BETTER) ├ Chunk 1 [512 tok]: "...entitled to a refund... unless product" └ Chunk 2 [512 tok]: "...refund... unless product was opened..." ✅ "unless product" appears in BOTH chunks. Either retrieval gives context. STRATEGY 3 — Semantic Chunking (BEST) ├ Chunk A: Full sentence group 1 (complete thought) └ Chunk B: Full sentence group 2 (complete thought) ✅ Never splits mid-sentence or mid-clause. Natural boundaries. STRATEGY 4 — Parent-Child ├ PARENT: Full paragraph (indexed for context) └ CHILD: Small sub-sentences (indexed for search) ✅ Retrieve child → Return parent → Full context always included
RECOMMENDED CHUNKING RULES
  • Never chunk mid-sentence — always break at sentence boundaries (periods, semicolons)
  • Keep related clauses together — "if...then," "unless...," "however..." clauses belong in the same chunk as their trigger
  • Use 10–20% overlap — share the last 1–2 sentences between adjacent chunks
  • Respect document structure — bullet points, numbered lists, and clauses are natural chunking boundaries
  • Test chunk quality — can a human read each chunk in isolation and understand it? If not, the chunk is too fragmented
Q123 SITUATION
Your RAG system takes 3 seconds. Business requires <500ms. Walk me through your optimization strategy.
STEP 1 — MEASURE BEFORE OPTIMIZING

You can't optimize what you haven't measured. First, instrument every step and find where time is spent:

Latency Breakdown Example (before optimization)
Total: 3,000ms ├── Query embedding: 200ms ├── Vector search: 150ms ├── Re-ranking (cross-encoder): 800ms ← BOTTLENECK! ├── Context assembly: 50ms └── LLM generation: 1,800ms ← BIGGEST BOTTLENECK!
STEP 2 — ATTACK EACH BOTTLENECK
ComponentSlow ApproachFast AlternativeSavings
LLM GenerationGPT-4 (1,800ms)GPT-4o-mini or streaming + perceived speed~1,000ms
Re-rankingCross-encoder on top-50Re-rank top-20 only, or use lighter model~500ms
Query EmbeddingAPI call (200ms)Local embedding model (20ms)~180ms
Vector SearchFlat exact searchHNSW approximate search~100ms
CachingNo cacheCache embeddings + final answers for repeated Qs~90% for cached
STEP 3 — PARALLELIZATION
Sequential vs Parallel Execution
SEQUENTIAL (3 seconds): [embed query] → [search] → [re-rank] → [assemble] → [LLM] 200ms 150ms 800ms 50ms 1800ms PARALLEL (saves ~600ms): [embed query] ──────────────────────────────▶ wait [metadata filter] ────────────────────────▶ wait Both finish → [merge results] → [LLM starts with streaming] Total: ~2,200ms already With streaming: user sees FIRST TOKEN at ~400ms — feels instant!
STEP 4 — SMART CACHING
  • Semantic cache: If query embedding is >0.95 similar to a recent query, return cached answer immediately (sub-10ms)
  • Embedding cache: Don't re-embed unchanged documents
  • Answer cache: FAQs and common queries → cache for hours
Reality: Getting from 3s to 500ms for a complex RAG system is very aggressive. Streaming (showing the first tokens immediately) can make 1.5s feel like 500ms to the user. UX and perceived speed matter as much as actual speed.
Q124 TRICKY
A user asks "What's the difference between Policy A and Policy B?" Your RAG retrieves documents about each separately, but the LLM can't compare them effectively. How would you redesign?
If you want to compare two cars, you need to see them side-by-side — not read a review of car A, wait, then read a review of car B. Your brain needs both in view simultaneously to compare. The LLM has the same problem when A and B are in separate document chunks without structure.
WHY THE LLM STRUGGLES WITH COMPARISON
  • Documents about A and B are retrieved and concatenated in the context without structure
  • The LLM doesn't know which paragraphs belong to Policy A vs Policy B
  • Context about Policy A and B are interleaved or separated by unrelated text
  • The LLM has to "mentally extract" information for each and compare — error-prone
REDESIGNED APPROACH — STRUCTURED COMPARATIVE RETRIEVAL
1
Detect Comparison IntentQuery classifier detects "compare X and Y" pattern. Switches to comparison retrieval mode.
2
Parallel Targeted RetrievalRun TWO separate retrievals simultaneously: search "Policy A" with filter=PolicyA_docs, and search "Policy B" with filter=PolicyB_docs. Don't mix them.
3
Structure the Context ClearlyPresent to LLM in a structured format:
"POLICY A: [relevant chunks]
POLICY B: [relevant chunks]
Question: What are the differences between them?"
4
Aspect-Based DecompositionPre-generate comparison dimensions: eligibility, coverage, cost, exclusions. Retrieve each dimension for both policies and fill a comparison table template.
5
Pre-built Comparison CacheFor frequently compared entities, pre-compute and cache the comparison at indexing time. First request is slow, all subsequent requests are instant.
Q125 SITUATION
After one week, response quality drops. No code or documents changed. What's happening?
Your restaurant hasn't changed its menu or chef, but customer satisfaction dropped this week. What changed? Maybe a new competitor opened, ingredients got worse (supplier issue), or customer expectations changed (new trend). Similarly, a RAG system can degrade without code changes.
POSSIBLE CAUSES (SYSTEMATIC DEBUGGING)
CategoryWhat ChangedHow to Diagnose
Data DriftUsers started asking different types of questions (query distribution shifted)Compare this week's queries vs last week. Has vocabulary, topic, or complexity changed?
External API ChangesLLM provider (OpenAI) silently updated their modelCheck provider release notes and changelogs from the past week
Index CorruptionVector database index partially corrupted or re-indexed with wrong settingsRun spot checks: retrieve known documents and verify they still return as expected
Rate LimitingIncreased traffic → hitting API rate limits → timeouts → fallback to worse modelCheck API error logs for rate limit responses (429 errors)
Embedding Model UpdateEmbedding service updated their model → new embeddings don't match old document embeddingsCheck if embedding model version changed. If yes, re-embed all documents.
Knowledge Base StalenessReal-world facts changed but documents weren't updated → correct answers now contradicted by outdated docsCheck if any indexed documents are now factually wrong due to recent events
Infrastructure IssuesMemory pressure, disk space, network latency → increased timeouts → worse LLM responsesCheck server metrics: CPU, memory, disk usage, latency percentiles
Prevention: Always monitor RAG quality continuously — not just at launch. Set up automated evaluation on a golden test set that runs daily and alerts if quality drops below threshold.
Q126 TRICKY
Your embedding model was trained on general web text. You're now deploying in a medical domain. What problems will you encounter?
Imagine asking a general librarian who knows English literature to manage a specialized medical library. They can read the books, but they don't understand that "MI" means "Myocardial Infarction" not "Mission Impossible," or that "positive" in cancer results means bad news, not good.
SPECIFIC PROBLEMS YOU'LL ENCOUNTER
ProblemExampleImpact
Term Ambiguity"Positive" = good in common language, bad in "positive biopsy"Retrieves wrong docs, wrong sentiment analysis
Acronym Blindness"MI", "CHF", "CABG", "PRN" have no general-language meaningAcronyms in queries fail to match spelled-out terms in docs
Latin/Greek Terms"Myocardial", "hepatic", "subcutaneous" — rare in web textLow-quality embeddings for medical terminology
Semantic Drift"Discharge" = hospital discharge vs electrical discharge vs gun dischargeWrong documents retrieved based on wrong sense
Dosage/Number Sensitivity"5mg" vs "50mg" — critical difference, but vectors may treat as similarWrong dosage information retrieved
Drug Name Similarity"Hydroxyzine" vs "Hydroxyurea" — very different drugs, similar spelling → similar vectorsDangerous confusion in drug information retrieval
SOLUTIONS
1
Domain-Specific Embedding ModelUse BioBERT, PubMedBERT, ClinicalBERT, or MedCPT — trained specifically on medical literature. These understand medical terminology natively.
2
Medical Ontology IntegrationIntegrate SNOMED CT, ICD-10, UMLS — standardized medical vocabularies that link synonyms ("MI," "heart attack," "myocardial infarction" → same concept).
3
Fine-Tune on Domain DataEven a general embedding model can be improved by fine-tuning on medical QA pairs from your specific use case.
4
Higher Retrieval Confidence ThresholdsIn medical contexts, it's safer to say "I don't know" than to retrieve a slightly wrong document. Raise similarity thresholds.
5
Expert ValidationHave medical professionals regularly review retrieval results and generation outputs. Automated metrics are insufficient for high-stakes domains.
Q127 SITUATION
A user asks a question requiring information from three documents, but each document alone doesn't contain the full answer. How does your RAG system handle this?
Solving a puzzle where piece 1 shows the top-left, piece 2 shows the bottom-right, and piece 3 shows the middle. No single piece shows the full picture. You need all three assembled together. Standard RAG just gives you the three pieces — you need a system that assembles them.
WHY THIS IS HARD FOR STANDARD RAG

Standard RAG retrieves documents, sends them to the LLM, and asks for an answer. If:

  • Doc 1 says "Employee X earns $90,000"
  • Doc 2 says "Senior employees get 15% bonus"
  • Doc 3 says "Employee X is Senior level"

Query: "What bonus does Employee X earn?" requires synthesizing all three. Standard RAG either misses docs or the LLM struggles to chain the reasoning.

SOLUTIONS FOR MULTI-HOP REASONING
1
Iterative Retrieval (Agentic RAG)Step 1: Retrieve for "Employee X salary" → find Doc 1. Step 2: LLM realizes it needs to know if X is Senior → retrieves Doc 3. Step 3: LLM realizes it needs bonus policy → retrieves Doc 2. Now synthesize: 90,000 × 15% = $13,500.
2
Question DecompositionLLM breaks complex question into sub-questions: "What is X's salary?" + "What level is X?" + "What's the bonus % for that level?" → retrieve for each → combine.
3
GraphRAGBuild a knowledge graph: Employee X → [is] → Senior → [gets] → 15% bonus. Multi-hop reasoning happens by traversing graph edges, not isolated document chunks.
4
Retrieve More DocumentsIncrease top-K from 5 to 15–20. Send all to LLM with instruction to "synthesize information across all documents to answer." Relies on LLM's reasoning ability.
Q128 TRICKY
User asks "How do I reset my password?" Your RAG retrieves 5 similar tickets, but the answer is in an FAQ with different wording ("password recovery"). How do you solve lexical mismatch?
You're searching for a book about "programming" in a library but it's filed under "software development." Both mean the same thing, but the library catalog doesn't know that. This is the lexical mismatch problem — same meaning, different words.
Lexical Mismatch: The user's words and the document's words are different, but the meaning is the same. Pure keyword search fails because there's no word overlap. Pure vector search may fail if the embedding model isn't sensitive to these synonyms.
SOLUTIONS TO LEXICAL MISMATCH
1
Better Embedding ModelA well-trained embedding model should map "password reset" and "password recovery" to very similar vectors (they're semantically the same). Test your model on such synonym pairs.
2
Query Expansion with SynonymsBefore searching, expand the query: "password reset" → also search "password recovery," "forgot password," "account recovery," "login help." Use WordNet, domain synonym lists, or an LLM to generate synonyms.
3
HyDE (Hypothetical Document)Ask LLM: "Write a FAQ entry that answers the question: How do I reset my password?" The LLM generates: "Password Recovery: To recover your account password..." — now search for docs similar to THIS generated text. It will match the FAQ because it uses the same vocabulary!
4
Add Synonym Metadata at Index TimeWhen indexing the FAQ, tag it with synonyms: "password recovery | password reset | forgot password | account recovery." These tags are included in the searchable text.
5
Feedback LoopWhen users mark an answer as unhelpful, log the query + the document they actually needed. Use this to build a synonym map over time.
Q129 SITUATION
Your RAG system hallucinates numbers even when correct numbers exist in retrieved documents. What's the likely cause?
THE SURPRISING TRUTH — LLMs ARE BAD AT NUMBERS

LLMs are trained on text, not math. Numbers in training data are treated as tokens, not as precise values. The model learned associations between "revenue" and "4-digit numbers" — not the exact meaning of specific numbers. Even when a document says "$4.2M revenue," the LLM may:

  • Correctly state $4.2M — fine
  • Say $42M (off by 10x) — "close" token but wrong value
  • Say $4.2B (unit error) — confused million with billion
  • Confuse this year's number with last year's from a nearby chunk
ROOT CAUSES SPECIFIC TO RAG
  • Multiple numbers in context: If 5 documents all contain different numbers, the LLM can mix them up
  • Number in table vs text: Numbers in tables are harder for LLMs to extract accurately than prose
  • Unit confusion: "4.2" appears both as "$4.2M revenue" and "4.2% growth" in different documents
  • Ambiguous reference: "The amount increased to..." — which amount? From which document?
FIXES
1
Structured Data Extraction Before LLMExtract numbers programmatically (not via LLM) using regex or rule-based extractors. Pass structured data: {"revenue_2024": "$4.2M", "unit": "USD"} to the LLM, not raw text.
2
Use NL2SQL for Numerical QueriesQuestions involving specific numbers → route to SQL database query, not LLM generation. The database returns exact numbers; LLM only formats them.
3
Numeric Verification LayerAfter LLM generates an answer, programmatically check: does every number in the answer appear in the retrieved documents? If a number in the answer isn't in the source docs → flag as potential hallucination.
4
Prompt Engineering"Extract and copy numerical values exactly as they appear in the documents. Do not perform arithmetic or convert units." Forces verbatim copying.
Q130 TRICKY
You're building RAG for a news archive. User asks "What happened on Jan 15, 2023?" Your system retrieves documents from that date but misses a story from Jan 14 that continued on Jan 15.
A developing story is like a TV show episode — you can't understand episode 3 without having watched episode 2. A news story that "continued from yesterday" needs context from the previous day to make sense. Pure date filtering misses this relationship.
THE TEMPORAL CONTINUITY PROBLEM

News stories often span multiple days. A date filter of "Jan 15" will retrieve:

  • ✅ "Earthquake strikes city — 45 dead" (Jan 15 article)
  • ✅ "Government responds to earthquake" (Jan 15 article)
  • ❌ "Death toll rising from yesterday's quake" (Jan 14, but continued into Jan 15)
SOLUTIONS
1
Extended Time WindowFor date-specific queries, search ±2 days around the specified date. A query for "Jan 15" should also retrieve Jan 13–17 articles, then let the LLM determine which are truly relevant.
2
Story Linking at Indexing TimeBuild a "story graph" at indexing time: detect when an article references a previous article ("continued from yesterday," "update: the earthquake that struck Tuesday"). Link these articles and retrieve connected articles together.
3
Continuation Marker DetectionUse NLP to detect "continuation signals" in articles: "yesterday's event," "ongoing," "update," "follow-up." Articles with these signals should link back to their predecessor articles.
4
Topic ClusteringCluster articles by topic and date. If querying Jan 15 and a topic cluster spans Jan 14–18, retrieve the full cluster. The earthquake cluster includes all related articles regardless of exact date.
Q131 SITUATION
Your RAG correctly answers 90% but fails badly on negation ("Which products are NOT eligible?"). Why does negation fail?
Imagine searching Google for "restaurants NOT Mexican" — but Google shows you Mexican restaurants because the word "Mexican" appears in your query. The search engine doesn't understand "NOT." Vector similarity has a similar blind spot.
WHY NEGATION FAILS IN RAG
  • Vector similarity ignores negation: "NOT eligible" and "eligible" produce very similar query embeddings — the negation word is a small modifier but doesn't flip the meaning in vector space
  • Retrieved wrong documents: Query "NOT eligible products" returns documents about eligible products (high similarity) — the opposite of what's needed
  • LLM doesn't apply negation to retrieved docs: The LLM sees retrieved docs about eligible products and ignores the "NOT" in the original query
  • Negation requires logical reasoning: "NOT in the list" requires comparing to an enumerated list, which is a logic operation, not a text retrieval operation
SOLUTIONS
1
Negation Detection and ReframingDetect negation in the query. Reframe: "Which products are NOT eligible?" → retrieve "eligibility policy" + "complete product list" → ask LLM to compute the complement (products NOT in eligible list).
2
Structured List QueriesInstead of vector search, retrieve the complete enumerated list from a structured source. Then apply set subtraction programmatically: all_products - eligible_products = ineligible_products.
3
Explicit Negation PromptingIn the LLM prompt: "First, identify what IS eligible from the documents. Then, from the complete product list, identify what is NOT in the eligible list. Report the difference."
4
Keyword Search for Negative TermsBM25 keyword search for "ineligible," "excluded," "not covered" — the document itself may use these terms. Augment vector search with keyword search for negation-specific vocabulary.
Q132 TRICKY
User asks a question. Retriever returns 10 chunks. LLM answers using chunks 1, 3, and 7. How do you debug if the answer is wrong?
SYSTEMATIC DEBUGGING FRAMEWORK
RAG Debugging Decision Tree
START: Answer is wrong Q1: Are chunks 1, 3, 7 the RIGHT documents? ├── NO → RETRIEVAL BUG │ ├── Was the relevant document indexed? → Check index completeness │ ├── Is the embedding model right? → Test with domain terms │ └── Improve chunking/embedding/hybrid search │ └── YES → GENERATION BUG │ Q2: Do chunks 1, 3, 7 CONTAIN the correct answer? ├── NO → CHUNK QUALITY BUG │ ├── Is answer split across chunk boundary? → Fix chunking overlap │ └── Is metadata wrong? → Fix document processing pipeline │ └── YES → LLM REASONING BUG ├── LLM misread the chunk → Simplify text, improve formatting ├── LLM ignored chunk 3 (Lost in Middle) → Reorder context ├── LLM hallucinated instead of reading → Lower temp, constrain prompt └── LLM chose wrong chunk → Conflicts between chunks 1, 3, 7?
OBSERVABILITY TOOLS TO MAKE DEBUGGING EASY
  • LangSmith: Traces every LangChain call — shows exactly what went into and came out of each step
  • Arize Phoenix: RAG evaluation and monitoring — identifies retrieval failures vs generation failures
  • Custom Logging: Log query, retrieved chunks (with scores), final answer, and user feedback for every request
  • Attribution: Ask the LLM to cite which chunk each sentence came from. If chunk 1 never gets cited → it wasn't useful for retrieval
Best practice: Build a "RAG evaluation harness" from day 1. For a golden test set of 100 known Q&A pairs, automatically check: (1) Was the correct document in the top-K? (2) Did the LLM use it? (3) Was the final answer correct? This lets you isolate where failures occur.
Q133 SITUATION
Your RAG uses a 7B model. Competitor uses 70B and gets better results. You can't afford the larger model. What RAG-specific optimizations can close the gap?
A highly organized research team with good processes (retrieval, structured context) can beat a lone genius with no organization. Similarly, a well-optimized RAG system with a smaller model can often beat a larger model with poor RAG architecture.
THE KEY INSIGHT

Larger models help with: complex reasoning, understanding ambiguous language, creative generation, multi-hop logic. But for RAG — where the answer is explicitly in the retrieved documents — much of that advantage can be compensated by better retrieval and context preparation.

OPTIMIZATIONS THAT CAN CLOSE THE GAP
OptimizationImpactDifficulty
Better Retrieval (Most Impact)Getting the right docs matters more than LLM size for factual Q&AMedium
Context CompressionRemove irrelevant sentences from chunks before sending — smaller, cleaner context helps small models more than largeMedium
Re-rankingPut the single most relevant chunk first — small models benefit more from good context orderingLow
Fine-Tune the 7B ModelFine-tune specifically on your domain's Q&A pairs — a domain-tuned 7B often beats a generic 70BHigh
RAG-FusionBetter retrieval via multi-query compensation for weaker reasoningMedium
Structured PromptsPrecise, structured prompts help small models more — they need clearer instructionsLow
Chain-of-ThoughtForce step-by-step reasoning in prompts — helps small models reason through complex questionsLow
Answer VerificationGenerate 3 answers, vote on the best (or check consistency) — compensates for lower single-pass reliabilityMedium
Research finding: A well-engineered RAG pipeline with a 7B model can match or exceed a 70B model with naive RAG implementation — especially on domain-specific factual tasks. Retrieval quality is the biggest lever in RAG performance.
Q134 TRICKY
Your RAG performs worse on follow-up questions ("What about the second option?" without context). How do you design multi-turn RAG?
Imagine calling customer support. First call: "Tell me about your premium plan." Agent explains. Second call (different agent): "What was the second option?" New agent has no idea what you discussed before — they need the history. Multi-turn RAG has the same problem: each query is independent without conversation memory.
THE MULTI-TURN PROBLEM

User turn 1: "What are the refund options?"
RAG retrieves and answers: "Options are (1) store credit (2) bank refund (3) exchange."

User turn 2: "What about the second option?" — Query sent to RAG:
❌ "What about the second option?" — has no context. RAG doesn't know what "second option" means. Retrieval fails.

SOLUTION — MULTI-TURN RAG ARCHITECTURE
1
Conversation History StorageStore all previous turns in a session context: [Turn 1: Q + A, Turn 2: Q + A, ...]. Keep last N turns (N=5-10) in memory.
2
Query Rewriting with HistoryBefore retrieval, use an LLM to rewrite the standalone query using conversation history:
Turn 2: "What about the second option?" + History → "Tell me more about the bank refund option" → now this query works for retrieval!
3
Contextual Compression of HistoryAs conversation grows long, compress older turns: "User asked about refund options. Answer: 3 options — store credit, bank refund, exchange." Keeps context window from overflowing.
4
Relevant History RetrievalFor very long conversations, embed each turn and retrieve the most relevant past turns (not just the last 5). If user returns to a topic from 20 turns ago, retrieve that exchange.
5
Entity TrackingTrack named entities across the conversation: "the second option" → resolved to "bank refund" → store in conversation state. Coreference resolution makes follow-ups work.
Multi-Turn RAG Pipeline
TURN 1: User: "What are refund options?" → Retrieve → Answer → Store [T1: Q1, A1] TURN 2: User: "What about the second option?" + History: [T1: Q1="refund options", A1="1. credit, 2. bank refund, 3. exchange"] ↓ QUERY REWRITER (LLM) Standalone Query: "Tell me more about bank refund option" ↓ RETRIEVAL (with good standalone query) → Retrieve relevant docs → Answer: "Bank refund takes 5-7 days..." → Store [T1:Q1/A1, T2:Q2_original/A2]
Q: What is the most important step in multi-turn RAG?
A: Query rewriting — converting a context-dependent follow-up question ("what about the second option?") into a self-contained, retrievable query ("tell me more about bank refund"). Without this step, retrieval completely fails.
Section 3 · Summary
Quick Reference — All 47 Questions at a Glance
Your cheat sheet for RAG interviews
Q#TopicOne-Line Answer
88What is RAG?Open-book AI: retrieve relevant docs, then generate grounded answers
89Benefits of RAGReduces hallucinations, always up-to-date, works with private data, citable
90RAG vs Fine-TuningRAG=external knowledge (update docs), FT=baked-in skill (update weights)
91Missing ContentVocabulary mismatch → fix with hybrid search + query expansion
92Missed Top DocsRanking imperfect → fix with re-ranking (cross-encoder) after initial retrieval
93Out of ContextChunking cuts meaning → fix with overlap, parent-child, sentence-window
94Extraction FailureToo broad/narrow → tune similarity thresholds, dynamic top-K
95Format ErrorsMessy source docs → clean with normalization pipeline before indexing
96Specificity ErrorsGeneral embeddings fail in domains → use BioBERT/LegalBERT/FinBERT
97Incomplete AnswersSingle-channel miss → multi-channel + multi-query retrieval
98Data ProcessingSlow extraction → parallel processing, OCR, Unstructured.io
99Structured DataRAG can't do SQL → add NL2SQL + query routing layer
100PDF ExtractionUse LayoutLM, Camelot, coordinate-level segmentation for complex PDFs
101Fallback ModelsPrimary fails → cascade to cheaper/simpler model → graceful degradation
102SecurityPrompt injection + privilege escalation → RBAC, sanitization, audit logs
103Why RAG-FusionOne query = one perspective → RAG-Fusion = 5 queries, fused results
104RAG-Fusion TechMulti-query generation + Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) algorithm
105RAG-Fusion WorkflowQuery → Generate 5 sub-queries → Parallel retrieval → RRF → LLM
106RAG-Fusion Pros/ConsMore complete but slower and more expensive than standard RAG
107OptimizationRetrieval: hybrid+rerank; Generation: compress+prompt; Storage: HNSW+cache
108Production ArchitectureAPI gateway + cache + retriever + LLM + observability + fallback chain
109Indexing OptimizationBetter embeddings + incremental updates (hash-based) + deduplication
110Data QualityNormalize, clean, remove boilerplate, add metadata before indexing
111Future of RAGMultimodal, GraphRAG, Self-RAG, Agentic RAG, continuous learning
112LimitationsRetrieval can fail, context limits, latency, still can hallucinate, security
113AdvantagesNo retraining, private data, citable, current, cost-effective, scalable
114Future IntegrationsGraphRAG + Self-RAG + AgentRAG + Multimodal RAG
115LLM Ignores Doc #3"Lost in the Middle" — put best doc FIRST in context; force reading all docs
11611th Doc Has AnswerRetrieve top-50, re-rank → or RAG-Fusion → or dynamic threshold top-K
117Name in SignatureEmbed metadata (author, sender) alongside content at indexing time
1185mg vs 10mg ConflictBoth RAG+LLM fail — pass dates in context, instruct to prefer recent, detect conflicts
119English Works, Spanish FailsMonolingual embeddings → use multilingual model or translate query first
12095% Recall, 60% SatisfactionAnswer quality, format, latency, UX, hallucination in generation all matter
12199% Accuracy ClaimAsk: test set source? accuracy definition? OOD questions? independent eval?
122Clause Split Across ChunksOverlap chunking + semantic chunking at natural boundaries + parent-child
1233s → 500msProfile first → cache semantically → parallelize → stream → lighter models
124Compare A vs BDetect comparison intent → parallel targeted retrieval → structured context
125Quality Drops After WeekCheck: query drift, API model updates, embedding model changes, rate limits
126General Embeddings in MedicineTerm ambiguity + acronyms + Latin terms → use BioBERT + medical ontologies
127Answer Needs 3 DocsIterative (agentic) retrieval + question decomposition + GraphRAG
128Lexical MismatchHyDE + query expansion + multilingual/domain embeddings + BM25 hybrid
129Hallucinated NumbersExtract numbers programmatically, use SQL for numbers, verify numbers post-gen
130Story Continues Next Day±2 day time window + story linking + topic clustering across dates
131Fails on NegationNegation reframing + structured list operations + keyword search for "not/exclude"
132Debugging Wrong AnswerWas right doc retrieved? → Did chunk have answer? → Did LLM use it? Tree of checks.
1337B vs 70B ModelBetter retrieval + context compression + fine-tuning + chain-of-thought prompts
134Multi-Turn RAGStore history + rewrite follow-up query to standalone → retrieval works again
You've Completed Section 3 — RAG Mastery!
