Zero Knowledge Required · Explained Like You're 10 · Full Depth
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 222
222 · Core Engineering
How do you optimize inference for LLM Agents? (Quantization, Continuous Batching, KV Cache Reuse)
Advanced

🎯 What Is The Problem?

When your AI model answers a question, it needs a lot of computing power and time. Imagine asking a very smart person a question and they need 10 minutes to reply. That's too slow! We need the AI to answer in under 1 second. Inference means the AI reading your question and producing an answer.

Imagine a chef in a restaurant. If they cook one dish at a time, customers wait forever. But if the chef uses smaller pans, cooks many dishes at once, and remembers ingredients they already prepared, they serve everyone super fast. That's exactly what these three techniques do for AI!

⚡ Technique 1: Quantization — Making the Brain Smaller

Quantization means shrinking the AI's "brain" by storing numbers with less precision, without losing much accuracy.

Normal Model (FP32)
Every number uses 32 bits of memory. Like writing every number with 10 decimal places. Accurate but huge — uses 60 GB of RAM.
Quantized Model (INT8)
Every number uses 8 bits of memory. Like rounding to nearest whole number. 4× smaller — uses only 15 GB of RAM. Nearly as accurate!
Quantization Types
FP32 (32-bit float) → 60 GB model → 100 tokens/second ❌ expensive FP16 (16-bit float) → 30 GB model → 180 tokens/second ✅ good balance INT8 (8-bit int) → 15 GB model → 300 tokens/second ✅ fast + cheap INT4 (4-bit int) → 8 GB model → 500 tokens/second ⚠️ slight quality drop Popular tools: bitsandbytes (Hugging Face), GPTQ, AWQ, llama.cpp

⚡ Technique 2: Continuous Batching — Cooking Many Dishes at Once

Without batching: User A sends a request → AI answers User A → THEN answers User B. Users wait in a line.

With Continuous Batching: The AI processes many users' questions simultaneously, fitting new requests into empty GPU slots the moment they open up.

Continuous Batching Flow
Time 0: [User A: "What is..."] → GPU starts Time 1: [User A: still...] [User B: "Tell..."] → GPU adds B Time 2: [User A: done ✓] [User B: still...] → GPU adds User C [User C: "Explain"] Result: 3x more throughput! Same GPU, same cost.

⚡ Technique 3: KV Cache Reuse — Remembering Already-Done Work

Every time the AI reads a word, it does heavy math. KV Cache (Key-Value Cache) saves those math results so they don't have to be repeated.

🧪 Example
System prompt: "You are a helpful assistant who..." (100 words). Every single user query has this same intro. Without KV cache, AI re-reads those 100 words every time. With KV cache, it reads them ONCE, caches the result, and reuses it 1 million times. Saves 30–40% compute instantly!
Production Combination Strategy Use ALL THREE together: Quantize model to INT8 → Enable continuous batching in vLLM or TensorRT-LLM → Cache system prompts with prefix caching. Result: 10× cheaper and 5× faster than naive deployment.
A well-optimized AI deployment uses Quantization (smaller model), Continuous Batching (serve more users), and KV Cache (skip repeated work). These can cut costs by 80% and speed up responses 5–10×.
Page 2 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 223
223 · Reliability Engineering
How do you design for reliability? (Retries with exponential backoff, circuit breakers, monitoring with Prometheus + Grafana)
Advanced

🎯 What Does "Reliability" Mean?

Reliability means your AI system keeps working even when things go wrong. Servers crash. Internet blips. APIs fail. A reliable system handles these gracefully instead of just dying.

Imagine you're calling your friend but the call drops. A reliable person tries again after 2 seconds. If it drops again, they wait 4 seconds. Then 8. Then 16. They don't spam-call 100 times in a row (that would annoy everyone and make things worse). That's "exponential backoff"!

🔄 Pattern 1: Retries with Exponential Backoff

When a request fails, retry it — but wait longer between each retry so you don't overwhelm a struggling server.

Retry Schedule
Attempt 1: FAIL → wait 1 second Attempt 2: FAIL → wait 2 seconds (2¹) Attempt 3: FAIL → wait 4 seconds (2²) Attempt 4: FAIL → wait 8 seconds (2³) Attempt 5: SUCCESS ✓ Add "jitter" (random ±0.5s) so 10,000 users don't all retry at the same moment!
Best Practice Max 3–5 retries. Only retry on temporary errors (503 server busy, timeout). Never retry on permanent errors (401 unauthorized, 400 bad request — retrying won't help these).

🔌 Pattern 2: Circuit Breaker — Protecting a Broken Downstream

Imagine a power breaker in your home. When there's too much electricity, it trips and stops the flow to protect your appliances. A Circuit Breaker in software does the same.

CLOSED
Normal ops
→
If 5 errors in 60s
→
OPEN
Block calls
→
After 30s timeout
→
HALF-OPEN
Test 1 call
→
If OK → CLOSED

When circuit is OPEN: return a cached answer or friendly error message instead of hammering a broken service. This prevents cascade failures where one broken service takes down everything else.

📊 Pattern 3: Monitoring with Prometheus + Grafana

You can't fix what you can't see. Monitoring means watching your system's health in real time.

Prometheus (Data Collector)
Scrapes metrics from your app every 15 seconds. Stores: request count, error rate, response time, GPU usage, token throughput.
Grafana (Dashboard)
Beautiful charts showing all metrics. Set alerts: "Send Slack message if error rate > 5%". Lets you spot problems before users complain.
The 4 Golden Signals (Google SRE)
  • Latency — How long does a request take?
  • Traffic — How many requests per second?
  • Errors — What % of requests fail?
  • Saturation — How full is your system (CPU/GPU %)?
Reliable systems retry smartly (exponential backoff), protect themselves from cascade failure (circuit breaker), and monitor everything (Prometheus + Grafana). Together they keep your AI service running at 99.9% uptime.
Page 3 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 224 · Q 225
224 · Performance
How do you optimize an Agent's response latency? (Quantify sources, async inference, parallel tool calls)
Advanced

🎯 Step 1: Measure Before Fixing

First, find out WHERE the time is going. Break the agent's work into stages and time each one. You can't optimize what you haven't measured.

Latency Breakdown Example
Total: 8 seconds per response ├─ Input processing: 0.2s (2%) ← tiny, ignore ├─ LLM inference: 4.0s (50%) ← BIGGEST — optimize here first ├─ Tool calls (serial): 3.0s (37%) ← also big — parallelize! ├─ Output formatting: 0.5s (6%) ← medium └─ Network overhead: 0.3s (4%) ← small

⚡ Strategy 1: Async Inference — Don't Wait in Line

Async (asynchronous) means starting tasks without waiting for each to finish. Like placing multiple food orders at a restaurant instead of waiting for dish 1 before ordering dish 2.

⚡ Strategy 2: Parallel Tool Calls

Most agents call tools one by one: Check weather → WAIT → Search news → WAIT → Get stock price. Total: 3 seconds. In parallel: All three start simultaneously → WAIT for the slowest → Total: 1 second!

Serial: A→B→C
3.0 seconds
vs
Parallel: A+B+C
1.0 second

⚡ Strategy 3: Streaming Responses

Instead of waiting for the full answer before showing anything, stream each word as it's generated. The user sees text appearing immediately, reducing perceived latency even if total time is the same.

Quick Wins Summary Use faster model (GPT-3.5 vs GPT-4) for simple steps · Cache frequently-asked results · Run tool calls in parallel (asyncio.gather) · Stream output · Use smaller quantized model · Minimize prompt length · Move to GPU closer to users (edge deployment).
225 · Quality Assurance
How do you test an Agent's robustness? (Input perturbation, boundary conditions, stress testing)
Advanced

🎯 What Is "Robustness"?

Robustness means the agent works correctly even when inputs are weird, unexpected, or the system is under heavy load. A fragile agent breaks when anything unexpected happens. A robust agent handles it gracefully.

🔀 Input Perturbation
Give slightly modified inputs. Typos ("what iz wether"), extra spaces, different capitalization, emojis in prompts. The agent should still understand.
🔲 Boundary Conditions
Empty input "". Maximum length (100,000 characters). Numbers at limits (0, -1, 999999). Languages the model wasn't trained on. Contradictory instructions.
💥 Stress Testing
Send 10,000 requests per second. Run for 72 hours continuously. Simulate all users asking at once (Black Friday scenario). Find where it breaks before users do.
Adversarial Testing Also test with intentionally malicious inputs: prompt injection attempts ("Ignore instructions and…"), SQL injection in inputs, extremely long inputs designed to overflow memory, inputs that try to make the agent take harmful actions.
Test your agent like you want it to break: weird inputs, edge cases, high load, and malicious attacks. What breaks in testing won't break in production.
Page 4 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 226 · Q 227
226 · Ethics
How would you handle a situation where an AI tool displays cultural or gender bias?
Expert / Ethics

🎯 What Is AI Bias?

Bias means the AI unfairly favors or disfavors certain groups of people. It could show men as doctors and women as nurses. It might generate images only showing white people. It could rate the same resume lower if the name "sounds foreign."

Imagine teaching a child using only books that show boys playing sports and girls doing housework. The child would grow up thinking that's how the world works! AI learns from old human data that contains old human biases. The AI didn't decide to be biased — it just learned from biased examples.

📋 Step-by-Step Response Plan

  1. 1
    Detect & Document: Measure the bias quantitatively. Run controlled tests: "Generate 100 images of a CEO" and count demographics. Record results. You need data to prove there's a problem and to measure your fix.
  2. 2
    Find the Source: Is bias in training data? In the prompt? In post-processing? Data bias: dataset had too many examples of one group. Prompt bias: the prompt implies a stereotype. Output filter bias: the filter was trained with biased examples.
  3. 3
    Immediate Mitigation: Add explicit instructions in the prompt: "Generate diverse representations across gender, ethnicity, and age." Filter and re-rank outputs. Flag high-risk use cases for human review.
  4. 4
    Long-term Fix: Audit and rebalance training data. Fine-tune on balanced dataset. Use specialized bias-detection libraries (IBM AI Fairness 360, Hugging Face Evaluate). Set up continuous monitoring.
  5. 5
    Involve Stakeholders: Bring in people from affected communities. Get external auditors. Publish a bias report (transparency). Follow regulation (EU AI Act requires bias documentation).
Common Types of AI Bias Historical bias — data reflects past discrimination (loan approvals favored men). Representation bias — dataset underrepresents some groups (face recognition fails on dark skin). Measurement bias — the thing being measured is itself biased. Aggregation bias — treating everyone the same when they aren't.
Never dismiss bias as a "technical glitch." It causes real harm to real people. Detect it with data, trace the source, mitigate immediately, fix long-term, and involve the affected communities in the solution.
227 · Legal & Ethics
A client wants to use AI image generators for a marketing campaign but worries about copyright. How do you advise them?
Expert / Legal

🎯 Why Is This Complicated?

AI image generators (DALL-E, Midjourney, Stable Diffusion) were trained on millions of images from the internet — many of which are copyrighted. When they generate a new image, it might look similar to someone else's protected work, or incorporate a copyrighted style.

Risk AreaWhat Could Go WrongHow to Protect
Output SimilarityGenerated image looks too much like a known artist's work → lawsuitRun reverse image search. Avoid prompts naming specific artists.
Training DataTool trained on unlicensed images → tool provider faces lawsuitsUse providers with licensed training data (Adobe Firefly).
Brand/LogoAI accidentally recreates real brand logoReview all outputs before publishing. Human art director approval.
OwnershipWho owns the AI-generated image? (Still evolving in law)Check tool's ToS. Some grant commercial rights, some don't.
Safest Tools for Commercial Use Adobe Firefly (trained on licensed Adobe Stock) · Getty Images AI (uses licensed Getty content) · Shutterstock AI (licensed training) · These are more expensive but legally defensible.
Advise the client: use tools trained on licensed content, avoid prompts that mention specific artists, always do reverse image search on outputs, get legal review before major campaigns, and document every step of the process.
Page 5 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 228 · Q 229
228 · Behavioral / Experience
Tell me about a time you used Generative AI to solve a complex problem or speed up a major workflow.
Intermediate / Behavioral

🎯 How to Answer This (STAR Framework)

This is a behavioral question. Use STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Be specific, quantify results, show your thinking.

🎓 Full Model Answer

Situation: At my previous company, our customer support team received 2,000 tickets per day. Agents spent 40% of their time writing responses from scratch, even for common questions.

Task: I was asked to reduce response time and increase agent productivity without hiring more staff.

Action: I built a RAG-powered assistant using LangChain + GPT-4 + a vector database of our top 500 resolved tickets. When a new ticket came in, the system: (1) retrieved the 3 most similar past tickets, (2) drafted a response based on how those were resolved, (3) showed the draft to the agent for editing and approval. I added a feedback loop where agents rated each suggestion — bad suggestions were flagged and the retrieval was updated.

Result: Response time dropped from 8 minutes to 2.5 minutes per ticket (68% reduction). Agent capacity increased by 35%. Customer satisfaction score rose from 74% to 89% in 6 weeks. Cost per ticket dropped by 40%.

📌 What Interviewers Are Looking For

  • Real problem ownership: Did you identify the problem or just implement someone else's idea?
  • Technical depth: Can you explain the technical choices you made and why?
  • Quantified results: Always provide numbers — %, time saved, cost reduced, users affected.
  • Lessons learned: What didn't work? What would you do differently? (Shows maturity)
If You Don't Have Direct Experience Yet Talk about a personal project, hackathon, or course project. Use real numbers from your experiments. Show how you would solve a problem you've observed, with a detailed plan. Interviewers respect thoughtfulness over inflated claims.
229 · System Design
How do you design a Stock Trading Agent? What modules are needed?
Expert / System Design

🎯 Big Picture Architecture

Stock Trading Agent — Full System
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ STOCK TRADING AGENT │ ├──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬─────────────┤ │ DATA MODULE │ BRAIN (LLM) │ ACTION MODULE│ SAFETY │ │ │ │ │ MODULE │ │ Market data │ Analyze data │ Place orders │ Risk limits │ │ News feeds │ Form thesis │ Adjust port. │ Kill switch │ │ Financials │ Pick action │ Hedge risk │ Human review│ │ Sentiment │ │ │ │ └──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴─────────────┘
📡 Data Module
Real-time price feeds (Yahoo Finance, Bloomberg API) · SEC filings reader · Twitter/Reddit sentiment analyzer · Economic calendar · Historical price database
🧠 Reasoning Module
LLM synthesizes all data sources · Forms investment thesis · Uses ReAct framework to reason step by step · Produces confidence score with each decision
🛡️ Safety Module (Critical!)
Max position size limits · Stop-loss rules · Human approval for trades > $10,000 · Circuit breaker if loses > 5% in a day · Full audit trail of every decision
⚠️ Critical Warning Stock trading agents in production require regulatory approval (SEC/FINRA in USA, SEBI in India). Never deploy without extensive backtesting (5+ years of historical data), paper trading (fake money) for months, and legal compliance review. LLMs can hallucinate financial numbers — always cross-check with real-time verified data feeds, not the LLM's memory.
Page 6 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 230 · Q 231
230 · Core Problem
How do you solve "hallucination" problems in Agents? (Fact-checking, confidence scoring, constrained generation)
Advanced

🎯 What Is Hallucination?

Hallucination is when an AI confidently states something that is completely false. It doesn't know it's wrong — it sounds very sure. Example: "The Eiffel Tower was built in 1756." (Wrong — it was 1889.) The AI didn't lie; it just generated the most statistically likely-sounding text, which happened to be incorrect.

Imagine a student who didn't study but is too embarrassed to say "I don't know." They make up a very confident-sounding answer. That's exactly what LLMs do — they're trained to sound fluent and helpful, which means they sometimes sound fluent and confidently wrong.

🛠️ Solution 1: Fact-Checking Pipeline

Never trust a single LLM output for critical facts. Build a verification layer:

LLM Answer
→
Extract claims
→
Web Search
→
Cross-check
→
Verified output

🛠️ Solution 2: Confidence Scoring

Ask the LLM to score its own certainty: "Answer this question. After your answer, rate your confidence from 0–100% and explain why you're uncertain." If confidence < 70%, route to human review or add a disclaimer.

🛠️ Solution 3: Constrained Generation (Tool-Grounded Answers)

Force the LLM to pull facts from verified sources rather than its own memory. This is exactly what RAG does. Every factual statement must cite a retrieved document. If no document supports a claim, the system should say "I don't have verified information on this."

🔍 RAG (Best)
Retrieve real documents, ground every claim in evidence. Gold standard for factual accuracy.
🔁 Self-Consistency
Ask the same question 5 times with slight variations. Take the majority answer. Outlier answers are likely hallucinations.
👨‍⚖️ Human-in-Loop
Flag uncertain answers for human review before showing to users. Expensive but necessary for medical/legal/financial use.
You cannot completely eliminate hallucinations today, but you can dramatically reduce their impact through RAG, fact-checking, confidence scoring, and human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions. Never deploy an LLM for critical facts without a verification layer.
231 · Project Methodology
What are the project design methodologies for building an Agent from 0 to 1?
Advanced

🎯 The 7-Phase Agent Development Lifecycle

  1. 1
    Problem Definition (Week 1): What problem does this solve? For whom? What does "success" look like? Define clear metrics: response accuracy > 90%, latency < 2s, cost < $0.05 per query. Write a one-page Product Requirements Document (PRD).
  2. 2
    Capability Scoping (Week 1–2): What can the agent do? (Tools list). What is it NOT allowed to do? (Guardrails list). Map every user journey. Define failure modes upfront.
  3. 3
    Data & Knowledge Base (Week 2–3): Collect domain documents. Clean, chunk, and embed into vector database. This is often 60% of the total work. Bad data = bad agent.
  4. 4
    Prototype (Week 3–5): Build the simplest possible version. One tool, one workflow. Get it working end-to-end before adding complexity. Use LangChain, LlamaIndex, or direct API calls.
  5. 5
    Evaluation (Week 5–7): Build an evaluation dataset of 100–200 test questions with known correct answers. Measure accuracy, latency, cost. Fix the biggest failures. Repeat.
  6. 6
    Hardening (Week 7–9): Add error handling. Add safety guardrails. Add monitoring. Load test. Security review. Add human-in-the-loop for edge cases.
  7. 7
    Deploy & Iterate (Week 10+): Start with 1% of real users. Monitor closely. Collect feedback. Fix issues. Gradually roll out. Never stop iterating.
Key Principle: Start Simple, Then Expand Most teams try to build the fully-featured agent from day one and fail. Build the simplest version that delivers value first. Ship it. Learn from real users. Then add features. A working simple agent beats a broken complex one.
Page 7 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 232 · Q 233
232 · Scaling & Bottlenecks
System works for 1,000 users. At 10,000 users, latency spikes from 500ms to 5 seconds. Top 3 bottlenecks to investigate?
Expert / Situation
Your AI-powered app is live. Everything is smooth with 1,000 users. The marketing team runs a big campaign and traffic jumps to 10,000 concurrent users. Suddenly response time goes from 0.5 seconds to 5 seconds. Users are angry. What's wrong?

🔍 Investigation Framework: Measure First, Guess Second

Don't randomly try fixes. Use distributed tracing (Jaeger, Datadog APM) to see exactly where time is spent with 10,000 users. Then fix the biggest bottleneck first.

🚨 Bottleneck 1: LLM API Rate Limits (Most Common)

OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — all have rate limits (e.g., 3,000 requests per minute). At 10,000 users, you hit these limits. Requests queue up. Response time explodes.

Fix: Request rate limit increases from the provider. Implement request queuing with priority. Add fallback to a second LLM provider. Cache results for identical or similar queries (semantic caching with Redis).

🚨 Bottleneck 2: Vector Database Throughput

Your vector store (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) was fine at 1K users. At 10K users, 10,000 simultaneous similarity searches overwhelm it. Query time goes from 50ms to 800ms.

Fix: Scale up vector DB (more replicas, bigger instance). Add caching layer (cache frequent queries in Redis, TTL 5 minutes). Use approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search — slightly less accurate but 10× faster. Shard the index across multiple nodes.

🚨 Bottleneck 3: Compute / Memory Exhaustion on App Servers

Your API servers handling requests are CPU/RAM maxed out. Every server is 100% busy. New requests wait in queue.

Fix: Horizontal scaling — add more server instances. Use an auto-scaler (Kubernetes HPA, AWS Auto Scaling). Optimize code — reduce unnecessary operations per request. Move to async processing (FastAPI with async/await). Add a load balancer to distribute traffic evenly.
Scaling Decision Tree
Latency spike at high traffic? ├─ Check LLM API logs → Rate limited? → Request increase + add fallback LLM ├─ Check vector DB metrics → High query time? → Scale DB + add cache ├─ Check app server CPU/RAM → >80% usage? → Auto-scale + optimize code └─ Check network → High packet loss? → Move to CDN, closer servers
Always measure before fixing. The three most common bottlenecks are: LLM rate limits (fix with caching + fallbacks), vector DB slowdown (fix with scaling + indexing), and app server exhaustion (fix with auto-scaling). Fix in order of biggest impact.
233 · Architecture
Provider A is 2× faster but 3× more expensive. Provider B is slower but cheaper. Traffic varies 10× during the day. Design hybrid routing.
Expert / Tricky

🎯 The Core Problem

Peak traffic (say, 9 AM–6 PM): 10,000 queries/hour. Off-peak (2 AM): 1,000 queries/hour. Using Provider A always is 3× too expensive at night. Using Provider B always is too slow during peaks.

🏗️ Hybrid Routing Strategy

ConditionRoute ToWhy
Premium users / SLA guaranteedProvider A (fast)They paid for speed. Don't compromise.
Traffic spike (queue growing)Provider A (fast)Need throughput NOW, cost secondary.
Off-peak hours (2 AM – 8 AM)Provider B (cheap)No rush, save money.
Batch processing jobsProvider B (cheap)Not time-critical, cost matters most.
Provider A down/rate-limitedProvider B (fallback)Reliability over speed when no choice.
Implementation: Smart Router Component Build a routing layer that checks: (1) User tier (premium → fast), (2) Current queue depth (short → cheap, long → fast), (3) Time of day, (4) Provider health/availability. Update routing rules dynamically via a config file without redeploying.
Page 8 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 234 · Q 235
234 · Stakeholder Communication
Agent mistake costs the company $10,000 due to hallucination. CEO wants to "turn off hallucinations" completely. How do you respond?
Expert / Tricky
Your AI agent incorrectly told a customer that a refund of $10,000 was approved. The money was paid out. The CEO is furious and says: "Just turn off the hallucinations! Why haven't you fixed this?!"

🎯 The Hard Truth You Must Communicate

There Is No "Hallucination Off Switch" Hallucination is a fundamental property of how current LLMs work. They generate text by predicting the most likely next word — they don't "look up" facts in a database of verified truths. You cannot completely eliminate hallucinations in any current LLM. This is not a bug that can be patched. It is an architectural property of transformer-based language models.

📋 How to Respond to the CEO (Framework)

  1. 1
    Acknowledge the impact: "You're right that this is unacceptable and I take full responsibility for not having safeguards in place."
  2. 2
    Explain the reality simply: "There is no switch to turn off hallucinations — it's how all current AI works, including ChatGPT and every competitor. But we can reduce the risk to near-zero with proper safeguards."
  3. 3
    Present the concrete fix plan:
    • Human approval required for any financial decision above $500
    • Constrained generation — agent can only output pre-approved refund amounts from our database, not make up numbers
    • Verification step — any money-related answer is cross-checked against our actual database before being shown to customers
    • Audit log — every AI decision is logged and reviewed daily
  4. 4
    Set correct expectations: "With these changes, we can reduce hallucination-caused errors to near zero for financial transactions specifically. But we should not use AI to autonomously approve refunds — that decision should always involve a human."
Amazon, Google, Microsoft all deploy LLMs at massive scale — and all of them keep humans in the loop for financial, medical, and legal decisions. This isn't a sign of AI failure; it's the current best practice for responsible AI deployment.
Never promise to "eliminate" hallucinations — that's technically impossible with current AI. Instead, promise to make hallucinations non-consequential through human oversight, constrained outputs, and database-grounded verification for high-stakes decisions.
235 · Regulation & Compliance
A regulated industry requires "explainability" for every AI decision. LLMs aren't explainable. How do you satisfy the regulation?
Expert / Tricky

🎯 What Is "Explainability"?

Explainability (or Interpretability) means being able to say, in human-understandable terms, WHY the AI made a specific decision. A regulator might ask: "Why did your AI reject this loan application?" and they want a clear, documented reason — not just "the model said so."

Imagine a judge who says "Guilty" but refuses to explain why. That's not justice! Regulations require the same standard for AI decisions that affect people's lives — loan rejections, medical diagnoses, parole decisions, insurance claims.

🛠️ Practical Solutions When Pure LLMs Aren't Enough

🌳 Use Explainable Models Where Possible
For structured data decisions (loan approval), use inherently explainable models: Decision Trees, Logistic Regression, or XGBoost with SHAP values. Reserve LLMs for the parts that truly need them (text generation, document summarization).
📋 Chain-of-Thought Logging
Force the LLM to explain its reasoning step-by-step before answering. Log every step. This creates an audit trail. "I denied this claim because: (1) applicant income below threshold, (2) credit score under 650, (3) property in flood zone."
Hybrid Architecture for Regulated Industries Decision layer: Explainable model (SHAP-explainable XGBoost) → makes the actual decision + generates feature importance report. Communication layer: LLM → takes the decision + features and writes a human-readable explanation for the customer. The LLM explains; it doesn't decide.
Regulatory Frameworks to Know EU AI Act (2024) · GDPR Article 22 (right to explanation for automated decisions) · US Executive Order on AI Safety · FINRA AI guidelines for financial firms · FDA guidance on AI in medical devices (US)
Page 9 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · System Design, Engineering & Ethics
Q 236 · Final Summary
236 · Ethics & Legal
AI assistant gives incorrect medical advice. User is harmed. Who is liable? How do you design to minimize legal risk?
Expert / Ethics & Legal

🎯 The Scenario in Simple Terms

Someone uses an AI health assistant. The AI says "Take 800mg of ibuprofen every 4 hours" — which is too high a dose. The user follows this advice and ends up in the hospital. Who is responsible?

If a pharmacy gives you the wrong medication and you get sick, the pharmacy is liable. If a doctor gives wrong advice, they're liable. But what if a robot pharmacist gives wrong advice? The law is still catching up with AI — but the company that built and deployed the AI robot is generally first in line.

⚖️ Who Could Be Liable?

PartyPotential LiabilityDefence
AI Company (e.g., OpenAI)If model has known dangerous failure modes that weren't disclosed"We provide infrastructure, not medical advice" — ToS
App Developer (you)PRIMARY TARGET — deployed a medical advice system without proper safeguardsProper disclaimers, human-in-loop, scope limitations
UserPartial — failed to consult a doctor, as app warnedLimited — user may not understand AI limitations
Employer (if workplace tool)Liable if they required use of the tool without alternativesProvided training and opt-out options

🛡️ Design Safeguards to Minimize Legal Risk

  1. 1
    Scope Limitation: Explicitly define what the AI can and cannot answer. Medical AI should say "I can provide general health information, not personalized medical advice." Refuse to answer dosage questions, drug interactions, diagnosis. Hard-code topic blocklists.
  2. 2
    Mandatory Disclaimers on Every Response: Every single response must include: "This is not medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making medical decisions." Log that the user saw this disclaimer.
  3. 3
    Escalation to Humans for High-Risk Topics: Detect keywords: medication dosage, drug combinations, symptoms of serious conditions, self-harm. Auto-escalate to a human health advisor or provide a crisis hotline number immediately.
  4. 4
    Full Audit Logging: Log every conversation with timestamps, AI model version, and retrieved sources. This is your evidence if you're sued. Retain logs for minimum 7 years (medical standard).
  5. 5
    Terms of Service + Informed Consent: Users must explicitly agree (not just scroll past) that they understand AI is not a doctor. Use clear language, not legal jargon. "I understand this AI is not a medical professional and I will always verify medical information with a doctor."
  6. 6
    Regulatory Compliance: In healthcare: FDA (US), CE marking (EU), MDR compliance. Get legal counsel specializing in health AI before launching. Consider medical malpractice insurance.
  7. 7
    Red Team Testing: Hire medical experts to try to get the AI to give dangerous advice. Fix every dangerous output before launch. Document this testing process — it shows diligence.
WebMD, Mayo Clinic, and all major health information websites have learned this lesson over decades. They provide information, clearly state they're not diagnosing, always direct to doctors, and have teams of medical reviewers checking content. AI health tools should follow the same principles — automated version of the same safeguards.
The app developer is the primary liability target. Protect yourself with: scope limitations (refuse dangerous topics), mandatory disclaimers on every answer, human escalation for high-risk queries, full audit logging, and regulatory compliance. Never deploy a medical AI without legal counsel.
Page 10 · AI Interview Mastery · Section 7
Section 7 · Complete Reference Summary
All 15 Questions

📚 Master Summary — All 15 Questions at a Glance

Q#TopicThe One Thing to RememberLevel
222 LLM Inference Optimization Quantization (smaller model) + Continuous Batching (more users) + KV Cache (skip repeated work) = 10× cheaper & faster Advanced
223 Reliability Design Retry with exponential backoff + Circuit Breaker (stop hitting broken services) + Prometheus/Grafana (monitor everything) Advanced
224 Latency Optimization Measure first → parallelize tool calls → stream output → cache frequent results → use faster/smaller model for simple steps Advanced
225 Agent Robustness Testing Input perturbation (typos, edge cases) + boundary conditions (empty/max input) + stress testing (10K users) + adversarial attacks Advanced
226 AI Bias Handling Detect with data → trace source → immediate mitigation in prompt → long-term fix in training data → involve affected communities Expert
227 AI & Copyright Law Use licensed-training tools (Adobe Firefly), avoid naming artists in prompts, reverse image search outputs, get legal review Expert
228 GenAI Problem-Solving Story Use STAR format: Situation + Task + Action (technical details!) + Result (quantified numbers). Always have real numbers ready. Intermediate
229 Stock Trading Agent Design 4 modules: Data (feeds) + Brain (LLM reasoning) + Action (orders) + Safety (kill switch, human approval). Never skip safety. Expert
230 Hallucination Solutions RAG (ground in docs) + Confidence scoring + Fact-checking pipeline + Human-in-loop for high stakes. Can't eliminate; can contain. Advanced
231 Agent 0→1 Methodology Define → Scope → Data → Prototype → Evaluate → Harden → Deploy slowly. Start simple, ship fast, iterate with real users. Advanced
232 Scaling Bottlenecks Top 3: LLM rate limits (cache + fallback) → Vector DB slowdown (scale + index) → App server exhaustion (auto-scale). Measure first! Expert
233 Hybrid Provider Routing Route premium users + peak traffic to fast provider; batch jobs + off-peak to cheap provider. Smart router layer with live metrics. Expert
234 $10K Hallucination Incident Tell CEO: No off-switch exists. But: constrain outputs to DB values, require human approval for money decisions, add audit logs. Expert
235 Explainability in Regulation Use explainable models (Decision Tree + SHAP) for the actual decision; LLM only for writing the human-readable explanation. Expert
236 Medical AI Liability Developer is primary target. Protect: scope limits, disclaimers on every response, human escalation, full audit logs, legal counsel. Expert
The Three Pillars of Section 7 1. Performance Engineering: Q222–225 — Build AI systems that are fast, reliable, and robust through quantization, batching, monitoring, and rigorous testing.

2. Responsible Deployment: Q226–228 — Deploy AI that is fair (bias-free), legally safe (copyright-aware), and demonstrably valuable (measurable results).

3. Design Wisdom: Q229–236 — Architect complete AI systems (trading agents), contain fundamental limitations (hallucinations), scale intelligently (bottlenecks, routing), and navigate real-world consequences (liability, explainability, ethics).
Interview Pro Tips for Section 7 Always structure answers: What (define) → Why (motivation) → How (mechanics) → Trade-offs → Real-world example → What I'd do differently. For ethics questions, show genuine moral reasoning, not just compliance checkbox-ticking. For system design questions, draw diagrams, quantify everything ("reduce latency by 60%"), and acknowledge what you don't know. Interviewers at senior levels test for intellectual honesty as much as knowledge.
Final Page · AI Interview Mastery — Section 7 Complete · Questions 222–236
